quick_facts.label: Indian occupational classification
quick_facts.value: India’s National Classification of Occupations (NCO) 2015 places this work in ISCO-08 unit group 2163, “Product and Garment Designers,” covering people who design and develop products for manufacture and prepare designs and specifications for mass, batch, or one-off production.

quick_facts.label: Core professional focus
quick_facts.value: For the physical-product interpretation of this node, the role combines user-centred product development with ergonomics, materials, manufacturing techniques, aesthetics, and project-based problem solving.

quick_facts.label: Typical senior-level contribution
quick_facts.value: Senior product-design work can include leading complex product development, developing prototypes to test concepts and manufacturing feasibility, supplying detailed input for manufacture, coordinating with manufacturing engineering, and coaching less-experienced designers.

quick_facts.label: Relevant Indian education examples
quick_facts.value: NID offers Product Design within its Industrial Design faculty at undergraduate and postgraduate levels; its B.Des. is a four-year programme and its M.Des. is a two-and-a-half-year programme. IIT Bombay’s IDC School of Design also offers M.Des. study in Industrial Design.

sections.heading: Responsibilities
paragraphs: A Senior Product Designer for physical products takes responsibility for translating user and market needs into products that can be specified and made. In the Indian NCO framing, product designers develop products for manufacture and prepare product designs and specifications for mass, batch, and one-off production.

At senior level, this normally means owning complex development work across concept definition, detailed design, prototypes and production readiness. A current employer role description for senior physical-product design specifically includes proof-of-concept and prototype builds to reduce technical or manufacturing risk, detailed design input for components and products, coordination with manufacturing-engineering teams, design-for-manufacture/assembly considerations, and coaching or mentoring designers.
bullets: Lead design decisions from research and concept development through prototype, design review, detailed specification and production hand-off.
Balance usability, ergonomics, appearance, material choice, manufacturing process, cost and feasibility rather than treating visual styling as a standalone task.
Coordinate design intent with engineering and manufacturing partners; communicate decisions, constraints and revisions clearly.
Raise team capability through critique, coaching, standards and mentoring.

sections.heading: Entry routes in India
paragraphs: A direct preparation route is a professional design education in Product Design or Industrial Design. NID’s Product Design curriculum explicitly covers tangible solutions for users and includes design principles, ergonomics, materials and manufacturing techniques; NID’s programme structure combines theory, skills, design projects and field experiences supported by studios and labs.

A postgraduate route is also available. NID lists Product Design as an M.Des. specialisation, while IIT Bombay IDC’s Industrial Design M.Des. develops product-design capability through projects and provides specialisation options including product detailing, product visualisation and advanced ergonomics. CEED is an entrance examination through which qualified candidates may apply to participating M.Des. programmes, subject to each institute’s own admission requirements.
bullets: Build a portfolio that demonstrates the full physical-product process: user or market insight, ideation, form development, ergonomics, material/process rationale, CAD or drawings, prototypes, testing, iteration and final production communication.
Use internships, sponsored projects, maker facilities or industry projects to gain evidence of working with real constraints and cross-functional stakeholders.
Treat specific entrance tests, eligibility criteria, application dates and seat counts as admission-cycle-dependent; verify them with the issuing institute before applying.

sections.heading: Skills to develop
paragraphs: The role needs a blend of design craft and technical judgement. NID identifies ergonomics, materials, manufacturing techniques, user needs and research as central to Product Design. IIT Bombay’s Industrial Design programme similarly emphasises relating technical and ergonomic functions to real human needs, with industry interaction during projects.

For senior physical-product work, capability in prototype-led learning and manufacturability is especially valuable. An employer description for the role calls for understanding manufacturing processes and construction methods, alongside management, coaching and mentoring support for designers.
bullets: Human-centred research and synthesis: turn observations, user needs and contextual constraints into a clear design brief.
Ergonomics, product form, usability and aesthetic judgement.
Materials, manufacturing processes, construction methods and design-for-manufacture/assembly thinking.
Visualisation and communication through sketches, models, CAD, prototypes, drawings, specifications and design reviews.
Systems thinking and collaboration with engineering, quality, supply-chain and manufacturing counterparts.
Leadership behaviours: give useful critique, explain trade-offs, organise work across projects and mentor designers.

sections.heading: Workplace and collaboration
paragraphs: This is generally an interdisciplinary product-development role rather than a purely studio-based design role. NID describes Product Design as addressing products, services and experiences through tangible solutions, and its programme structure includes field experiences and sponsored projects. IIT Bombay’s Industrial Design programme likewise describes interaction with industries, institutions and social organisations during projects.

In practice, the mix of desk work, workshop or lab activity, prototype evaluation and manufacturing engagement depends on the sector and employer. The senior product-design employer example assigns coordination with manufacturing-engineering teams, prototype and feasibility work, and support for projects delivered on time and to cost targets.
bullets: Common collaboration partners include industrial or mechanical engineers, manufacturing engineers, prototype shops, quality teams, suppliers, product or business stakeholders and junior designers.
Work outputs can range from research and concept presentations to CAD data, physical prototypes, component details, specifications and production-facing design information.
Site, factory or supplier engagement may be needed where product feasibility, tooling, assembly or quality decisions must be resolved.
